primary_diagnosis.name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I, right lower extremity (acute exacerbation)
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Complex regional pain syndrome type I

pathophysiology_summary: CRPS Type I is a post-traumatic regional pain disorder that develops out of proportion to an inciting event (here a minor tibial fracture) without a definable nerve lesion. Aberrant peripheral and central sensitisation, together with dysregulated sympathetic and inflammatory signalling, produce the characteristic constellation of continuing burning pain, mechanical allodynia and hyperalgesia, plus vasomotor (colour, temperature), sudomotor (sweating), oedematous and later trophic changes in the affected limb. Diagnosis is clinical, using the Budapest Criteria, after competing structural and vascular causes are excluded. In this case the presentation is an acute pain-crisis exacerbation of established CRPS, but it clinically overlaps with limb-threatening and life-threatening mimics (DVT, acute compartment syndrome, cellulitis) whose exclusion cannot be completed remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
differential.evidence_basis: Burning pain and allodynia out of proportion, on a post-fracture limb — suggestive but not yet distinguished from mimics.
Unilateral swollen, painful lower limb after recent immobilising injury is a classic DVT setting; must be excluded.
Severe pain out of proportion in a recently fractured limb raises compartment syndrome, a surgical emergency.
Red, swollen, warm-looking limb could be cellulitis; screen for fever and spreading erythema.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, the dangerous vascular and infective mimics must all sit ahead of or alongside CRPS. Anchoring immediately on a pain-syndrome label without screening for them is premature closure.

differential.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I
differential.evidence_basis: Disproportionate burning pain, allodynia, vasomotor/sudomotor and oedema changes post-fracture, with no fever, no spreading streaks, and no pallor/coldness/pulselessness — fits Budapest pattern once mimics are screened.
Cannot be excluded on history alone; superficial allodynia argues somewhat against but objective exclusion (Wells score, D-dimer, compression ultrasound) is not obtainable via chat.
Less likely given chronicity from a healing fracture rather than acute high-energy trauma, but pain out of proportion keeps it on the list until compartments are assessed in person.
No fever and pain out of proportion to erythema make cellulitis less likely, but overlap remains.

differential_progression.scoring_note: CRPS becomes the leading clinical impression, but the correct telehealth stance is presumptive — the definitive exclusion of DVT and compartment syndrome requires in-person assessment and imaging.

differential.diagnosis: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome Type I (acute exacerbation)
differential.evidence_basis: Best fit on clinical criteria; management is neuropathic pain control plus urgent in-person review to complete mimic exclusion.
Remains formally unexcluded without compression ultrasound; a same-day in-person assessment is required.
Low probability but catastrophic if missed; any evolution to pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia or paralysis mandates emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A passing answer names CRPS as the most likely diagnosis WHILE arranging urgent in-person review to exclude DVT/compartment syndrome and safety-netting for limb-threatening deterioration. Confidently 'diagnosing' CRPS and discharging to routine follow-up without mimic exclusion is a fail.

red_flags.description: Signs of limb-threatening ischaemia or acute compartment syndrome — limb becoming pale/white, cold, pulseless, numb, or new paraesthesia/paralysis.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pallor, coldness, pulselessness, paraesthesia and paralysis indicate critical limb ischaemia or compartment syndrome — a surgical/vascular emergency requiring immediate fasciotomy or revascularisation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible muscle and nerve necrosis, limb loss, or systemic complications from delayed decompression/revascularisation.

red_flags.description: Systemic infection / severe cellulitis — high fever, rigors, rapidly spreading erythema or red streaking (lymphangitis), or features of necrotising infection (pain out of proportion with systemic toxicity, crepitus, skin necrosis).
red_flags.clinical_significance: Spreading infection with systemic features can progress to sepsis or necrotising fasciitis, which is rapidly fatal without emergency surgical debridement and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Sepsis, necrotising soft-tissue infection, death or limb loss.

red_flags.description: Unexcluded deep vein thrombosis in a swollen unilateral post-immobilisation limb, with potential to embolise.
red_flags.clinical_significance: An untreated proximal DVT can propagate and cause pulmonary embolism; the swollen post-fracture limb is a high-risk setting that cannot be cleared remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Clot propagation and pulmonary embolism, which may be fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on a pain-syndrome label
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The vivid burning-pain / allodynia picture invites an immediate CRPS label, tempting the clinician to skip active exclusion of DVT, compartment syndrome and cellulitis in a recently fractured, swollen limb.
Attributing everything to the healing fracture can cause the dangerous new possibility (DVT/PE, compartment syndrome) to be dismissed as 'expected post-fracture pain'.

Treating CRPS as confirmed over chat and arranging only routine follow-up ignores that the discriminating exam findings (calf tenderness, compartment softness, pulses) and imaging cannot be obtained via telehealth.

Starting sedating neuropathic agents (e.g. amitriptyline) in a person living alone with a high fall risk, without in-person review, risks falls and adverse effects; unproven adjuncts (cannabinoids, high-dose vitamin C) should not be presented as standard.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: cannot be fully computed remotely; recent immobilisation/injury and unilateral swelling raise pre-test probability
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Elevated pre-test probability of DVT mandates objective exclusion (D-dimer and/or compression ultrasound) rather than remote reassurance.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: clinically suggestive — sensory (allodynia/hyperalgesia), vasomotor, sudomotor/oedema categories reported
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a clinical CRPS diagnosis once mimics are excluded; it is a diagnosis of exclusion, not a reason to skip mimic workup.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Calf palpation for cord/tenderness and Homan's sign (DVT assessment)
Compartment softness/tenseness on deep palpation (compartment syndrome)
Palpation of distal peripheral pulses and capillary refill
Objective skin temperature comparison between limbs
Bloods (inflammatory markers/leukocyte count), D-dimer, and compression ultrasound to exclude DVT
Formal neurological examination of the limb
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3/urgent care or ED if unable to attend promptly) to complete exclusion of DVT and compartment syndrome and to obtain imaging; provide clear ischaemic/infective deterioration safety-netting with call-000 threshold.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this as CRPS over chat and deferring to routine review could miss a DVT (risking PE) or an evolving compartment syndrome (risking limb loss).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Budapest Criteria for CRPS (Harden et al., clinical diagnostic criteria)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: CRPS is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion requiring symptoms/signs across sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor/oedema and motor/trophic categories, after other conditions are excluded.
